source_paper_title: Tight Running Times for Minimum ℓq-Norm Load Balancing: Beyond Exponential Dependencies on 1/ϵ
context:
Page 2, Introduction (end of Contribution Overview paragraph); also reiterated in Page 46, Section H (Conclusion)

It remains an problem to obtain tight running times when m = Θ((1/ε)^θ) for θ ∈ (1/2, 1].

Another interesting problem is to show subexponential lower bound or develop FPTAS for P||P_i C_i^q where m = Θ((1/ε)^θ) for θ ∈ (1/2, 1].
title: Tight PTAS running times for P||\sum_i C_i^q when m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta), \theta\in(1/2,1]
latex:
Given constants \(q>1\) and \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\), consider the identical-parallel-machine scheduling problem \(\mathrm{P}||\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\): there are \(n\) jobs with processing times \(p_1,\dots,p_n\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}\) and \(m\) identical machines. A schedule is an assignment of each job to exactly one machine, and the load of machine \(i\) is \(C_i=\sum_{j\to i} p_j\). The objective value is \(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\), and \(\mathrm{OPT}\) denotes the minimum objective value over all schedules.

For a given accuracy parameter \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), a \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximation algorithm (PTAS) outputs a schedule with objective value at most \((1+\varepsilon)\mathrm{OPT}\) in time \(T(n,m,\varepsilon)\).

Assume the machine count scales with \(\varepsilon\) as \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for a fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\). Determine the asymptotically tight dependence of the minimum possible running time \(T(n,m,\varepsilon)\) of a PTAS for \(\mathrm{P}||\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\) as a function of \(1/\varepsilon\) (up to \(\mathrm{poly}(n)\) factors), under standard complexity assumptions (e.g., ETH):
- either give a PTAS with running time \(\mathrm{poly}(n)\cdot f(1/\varepsilon,\theta)\) and a matching (or near-matching) lower bound \(\mathrm{poly}(n)\cdot g(1/\varepsilon,\theta)\),
- or otherwise characterize the correct exponent/subexponent behavior in \(1/\varepsilon\) for this regime of \(m\).
location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (end of Contribution Overview paragraph); also reiterated in Page 46, Section H (Conclusion)
open_problem_quote:
It remains an problem to obtain tight running times when m = Θ((1/ε)^θ) for θ ∈ (1/2, 1].

Another interesting problem is to show subexponential lower bound or develop FPTAS for P||P_i C_i^q where m = Θ((1/ε)^θ) for θ ∈ (1/2, 1].
